title: Time Restricted Eating in Alzheimer's Disease (TREAD): A Pilot Study

summary.hook: This poster describes a pilot study (“TREAD”) testing whether a time-restricted eating (TRE) intervention is feasible in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and exploring effects on cognition and blood biomarkers relevant to Alzheimer’s disease (AD).
summary.summary_30s: This poster describes a pilot study (“TREAD”) testing whether a time-restricted eating (TRE) intervention is feasible in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and exploring effects on cognition and blood biomarkers relevant to Alzheimer’s disease (AD). In preliminary results, 27 participants completed the study and adherence to the targeted eating window was high (e.g., an 8-hour window achieved on 78.2% of days). The authors conclude the intervention appears feasible and that completing remaining participants will clarify impacts on cognition and AD-related blood biomarkers.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Investigate the feasibility of implementing a TRE intervention in patients with MCI

Approach: Population: Adults with MCI (Mayo Clinic criteria), ages 55–89 years, BMI >18.5 and <40.0 kg/m², with a supportive study partner Primary outcome: Feasibility/adherence (implied primary based on aims and “Eating Windows Achieved”)
Finding: Completion: N \= 27 completed participants (17 men, 10 women).

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: “Preliminary observations from the TREAD trial indicate high feasibility and adherence to a TRE intervention among patients with MCI.” Also: “Completion of the remaining participants will shed light on the potential impact of TRE on cognition and blood biomarkers of AD.” Implications (poster-grounded): The poster supports that implementing TRE with monitoring/support is feasible in this MCI sample and that further completion/analysis is needed to assess cognitive and biomarker effects. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Fasting leads to ketone production and metabolic switching from glucose to fatty acid/ketone utilization, triggering adaptive metabolic/cellular responses; additionally, cortical glucose uptake is decreased in AD while ketone uptake is not affected. Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): “Cortical glucose uptake is decreased in Alzheimer’s disease (AD); ketone uptake is not affected.” “Little is known about the potential benefits of TRE in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI).” (No citations/references listed on poster.)
key_numbers: TRE intervention described as “5 days/week x 12 weeks”
Adults with MCI (Mayo Clinic criteria), ages 55–89 years, BMI >18.5 and <40.0 kg/m², with a supportive study partner
8h window achieved on 78.2% of days; 10h on 94.7% of days
Exit interview: 93% benefited; 95% could incorporate long term; 74% said 16h fast not too long; 85% would recommend
takeaways: Completion: N \= 27 completed participants (17 men, 10 women).
Adherence: 8h eating window achieved on 78.2% of all days; 10h eating window achieved on 94.7% of all days.
Eating-window distribution: Percent of days meeting thresholds: ≤8h 78.2%; ≤9h 90.6%; ≤10h 94.7%; ≤11h 97.8%; ≤12h 99.0%. Goal marker shown: 5/7 days/week (71%).
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What is the planned total enrollment (and how many participants remain to complete)?

what_to_ask.question: In Figure 3, what explains the difference between achieving ≤8h on 78.2% of days vs ≤10h on 94.7%—were most “misses” modest overages?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: What feasibility criteria were prespecified (e.g., ≥71% of days meeting target, retention targets, safety)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: Based on feasibility, what level of support (RD time, devices) seems necessary to replicate adherence in clinics?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
